critique.summary: The article introduces Ɛpsilon, a digital platform developed by Cambridge University Library and partners to aggregate and make searchable 19th-century scientific correspondence. It aims to facilitate new research by consolidating over 50,000 letters from various sources, including those of prominent and lesser-known figures, thereby enabling a broader understanding of scientific communication during the long 19th century.
mainPoints: Ɛpsilon is a digital platform that collects and cross-searches 19th-century scientific correspondence.
It currently holds details of over 50,000 letters and is continuously growing.
The platform helps researchers access scattered and rarely item-level catalogued correspondence collections.
It includes letters from well-known scientists like Darwin, Faraday, and Wallace, as well as lesser-known figures.
The project is developed by Cambridge University Library's Darwin Correspondence Project in partnership with other academic and archival institutions.
Funding is secured to support the platform's long-term sustainability.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are accurate and verifiable. Cambridge University Library's Darwin Correspondence Project is a well-documented initiative, and the existence of a digital platform like Ɛpsilon aligns with current academic efforts to digitize and make accessible historical scientific correspondence. The details about the number of letters, contributors, and the project's goals are consistent with publicly available information from Cambridge University and related scholarly sources.
considerations: The article does not mention any potential limitations or challenges related to the completeness or digitization quality of the correspondence collections.
It does not discuss user accessibility or the technical requirements for using the platform.
There is no mention of how the platform handles copyright or privacy issues related to the letters.
The article does not provide perspectives from users or researchers who have employed the platform in their work.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the exploration of 19th-century scientific correspondence, highlighting the mix of formal and informal content, the humanizing aspects of historical figures, and changes in letter-writing style over time.

comments.author: doodlebugging
comments.text:
Interesting stuff. It is strange to me seeing how patronizing some of the correspondence ends up being with all the profuse thanks to the recipient for taking them in, under their wing, being a friend, etc. The subject matter in some ranges from scientific, to reminders of payments due, to questions in French about whether the Italian women are as attractive as English women.

For example: Letter from Benjamin Abbott to Michael Faraday, 1814.[0]

[0]https://epsilon.ac.uk/view/faraday/letters/Faraday0039

Faraday's reply to Abbott, 1815 [1]

[1]https://epsilon.ac.uk/view/faraday/letters/Faraday0046

Both comments are at the tail of the letter in French and their translation is in the footnotes.

I think Faraday was pulling Abbott's leg here so that he wouldn't be inclined to travel south to find out for himself. Like a fisherman would in protecting his favorite fishing holes.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced observation about the tone and content of the letters, supported by specific examples and plausible interpretation of historical context.

comments.author: robot-wrangler
comments.text:
Found nothing I was actually looking for, but also found neat/weird stuff by accident almost immediately. For example here's some incomplete record of Darwin and Lewis Carrol[1] passing notes. I'm sure much of the corpus is trivial like that, but always fascinating to see legends humanized.

[1]: https://epsilon.ac.uk/search?keyword=dodgson&f1-addressee=Do...
comments.analysis: The comment reflects a genuine user experience of discovery and highlights the value of the archive in humanizing famous figures, which is a reasonable and accurate insight.

comments.author: trebligdivad
comments.text:
Loads of random notes from/to and about Babbage.
When did letter writing like this slim down and all the long flowery language disappear?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid historical question about the evolution of letter-writing style, though it is more speculative and less detailed than others.
